title: Window Functions, Analytical Grain, and Time-Aware SQL · Changed-Data Lab
summary: Service leaders need rankings, running effort, previous-event comparisons, and rolling metrics without collapsing detail rows. The scenario, data, constraints, and failure modes differ from the workshop. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of a time-aware service-level analytics report with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define window partition, order, and frame independently from final result ordering.
Choose ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, and NTILE with explicit tie semantics.
Use LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, and LAST_VALUE with deliberate ordering and frame behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.sql:
-- sql-basics cumulative relational data portfolio.
-- Each increment declares grain, predicts cardinality or changed rows, runs in fresh browser SQLite,
-- reconciles results, tests changed data, and records PostgreSQL or standard-SQL boundaries.

SELECT sqlite_version() AS browser_sqlite_version;

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab Time Semantics
steps.instruction:
Append a runnable SQLite increment headed "-- Evidence: sql-time-semantics-i56em4" with a concrete grain, hypothesis, or prediction; window partition, ordering, frame, rank, or time evidence; and an observation, reconciliation, or verification tied to returned rows, changed rows, an error, or a plan.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab Time Semantics.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Time Semantics into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab Time Semantics, keep Time Semantics connected to executable relational evidence.

content.code:
-- Evidence: sql-time-semantics-i56em4
-- grain: declare the exact row or mutation grain before running
SELECT ticket_id, team_id,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY team_id ORDER BY opened_at, ticket_id) AS arrival
FROM tickets ORDER BY team_id, arrival;
-- observation: reconcile the returned rows, changed count, error, or plan with the prediction
